103336abaf14:
Escorting comes first: an enemy ship sighted within nine tiles of our fishing
boats, transports or fisheries gives the fleet an intercept point.
525a8e995248:
Sea control: a strong enough fleet shells the shore, blockades the fords a land
army has to cross, and hunts the enemy's fisheries.
At the highest difficulties this doubles as a second front -- while the main army
is committed, the fleet presses at the point furthest from it, and the threshold
for acting drops so that parity is enough to try. Lower difficulties keep the
safety margin and shell only near their own coast.
205582479248:
With nothing to do, patrol our own fishery zone; with no fishery, sit by the
dock and repair.
905ee33087d4:
Island grabs by transport: a multi-step state machine that abandons the mission
if any phase stalls for a minute, so it cannot loop forever.
968c9c0bd5ef:
============ 9c. AI military brain ============
The AI's army is produced by its own barracks, stables and workshops through the
player command path -- unlock, age, starvation and cost gates all apply -- and it
pays food upkeep like any army, so cutting off its food really does starve it.
Its jobs are route analysis (map-agnostic: several alternative paths, the
chokepoints on them, and a staging point, with no hard-coded coordinates),
composition (countering what the enemy actually fields), and the attack/defend
state machine.
f67469e54968:
Brain state is per side rather than hard-coded to one, which is the precondition
for running whole games of AI against AI.
b307ca128775:
Difficulty knobs: three fair tiers and three handicapped ones. Every constant in
the brains reads from this table.
A handicap only ever changes numbers that are disclosed on screen -- a gathering
multiplier, a cap on attack/defence upgrades. It never bypasses placement rules,
costs or the fog of war.
8aa4cab8c04b:
Army size has two separate axes, and conflating them was a real bug: one number
meant both "how big an army this difficulty aims for" (fair, and true at every
tier) and "multiply it because this tier is handicapped" (disclosed only on the
handicapped tiers). Written as one knob, a fair tier was quietly getting a
handicap it never declared.
3900eafc02b9:
adapt = how much the main force scales with the enemy army that has actually been
scouted. Without it the cap depended only on the state machine, so falling behind
meant training less -- a death spiral. Easy difficulties do not read enemy
strength at all; hard and above scale both composition and size to it.
84a27946cf29:
bind = how tightly personality is bound to civilisation. At the lowest difficulty
the draw often mismatches (a heavy-armour civilisation sent out to harass), which
is part of what makes it read as unfocused; from hard upwards it always matches.
The research line always follows the real civilisation -- personality only shapes
behavioural style.
da00a1a7e318:
The tactical columns: squads = how far the army can be divided (one main force
and a fixed guard at the bottom, plus patrols, plus mission squads at the top);
cmpFoe = whether to compare strengths before attacking rather than only counting
waves; tgtSel = how a target is chosen.
2565b90b5adc:
Personality is read from the civilisation table, so adding a civilisation adds a
row rather than a branch. The technology line always follows the actual
civilisation; only the behavioural columns (opening, harassment, retreat, expansion
appetite, walling) come from the personality draw.
2ae4a014c844:
Villager target = the map's carrying capacity (gatherable amount plus renewable
caps) scaled by a per-difficulty coefficient, rather than a fixed head count.
The dark age gets 0.72 of it, because an early economy cannot absorb a full crew.
The outpost cap is the number of ore clusters outside the radius times a
per-difficulty willingness -- non-zero even at the easiest setting: everyone
expands, the easiest just wants to least.
6fda4f8491d9:
March coefficient: every time-based parameter is shifted globally by how long it
takes to march between the two bases.
Before the route graph exists (the opening is still fogged) it is estimated from
static terrain along a straight line, weighting slow ground and treating deep
water as a detour. Once real routes are computed, the actual path length replaces
the estimate -- a winding map is not its straight-line distance.
e9fb45051d4c:
Estimating enemy strength. The old code took whatever enemy units were visible
right now, which turned scouting into a pure penalty: an AI that never scouted
believed the enemy had nothing and escalated all the way to an all-in attack,
while one that sent scouts saw the truth and stayed cautious -- and lost to it.
The replacement is an uncertainty model: observations decay, and underneath them
sits a prior for how large an army is likely to be at this point in the game.
ca0c1ee82dbe:
The prior curve, taken from measured army-strength curves in simulation.
An over-optimistic prior rewards blindness: the side that assumes an empty map
commits everything, and the side with scouts sees the truth and hesitates. The
prior has to approximate what an opponent really has by now, so that not scouting
is honestly uncertain rather than accidentally confident.
ea99c3f3b90d:
Staged marching. Attack-moving from home to the enemy base end to end means fast
units arrive first and siege engines last, strung out and defeated in detail.
Instead the force gathers at a staging point short of the first chokepoint: the
centroid arrives, and the next leg waits until a set fraction of the attacking
units is inside the gather radius -- with a timeout, so a few dead stragglers
cannot stall the whole attack.
c85fd825b9df: Constants for attacking at the right moment, with ablation switches.
1105f8026aae:
Actuator fixes -- commit to a pounce, latch the gather, keep medics with the
army -- with ablation switches. All of them go through existing commands and add
no new source of randomness.
2cfdc53c5390:
Tactical actuators: kiting, garrisoning troops, live patrols, towers shooting
buildings. Hard difficulty and above.
53d9be4b6b07:
Siege coordination and medical support: escort synchronisation, falling back when
cut off, a medic production line, and pulling badly wounded units out to heal.
62b26ce26492:
Breaking into a defended base.
Probing showed that the invisible wall attackers hit about thirty tiles out was
not an ambush -- it was the defender's forward ring of outposts and towers.
Marching straight at the command centre walks into the thickest overlap of three
towers at once, so the attack now works the ring apart a point at a time.
d7713a30ae59:
The three-squad brain -- home guard, attack force, reserve -- which replaced a
much more elaborate scoring state machine with base swaps, retreat-into-attack
flips, blockades and feint squads.
The home guard stays back as a buffer alongside the towers and the town centre's
fire; the attack force leaves and does not retreat; the reserve accumulates the
next wave.
8cb2ac70a5a6:
The launch threshold is tied to the all-in army size, so it is by construction
reachable: the same cap governs production, meaning the threshold can never
exceed what the AI is able to build. It grows with the economy, the enemy and the
difficulty.
3483e9ce12ef:
Strategy layer: choosing which town to attack, gathering before arrival, fighting
what is met on the way (running into an army on the road is a fight, not a reason
to go home), changing target mid-march at higher difficulties, roaming scouts and
a stalemate breaker. Each has its own ablation switch.
c5663e3e0a45:
Economic support for the three-squad brain: an even early split between military
and economic production (probing convicted a late military start of the
ten-minute first wave), wave size scaling with the age, renewable building keeping
pace with villagers, outpost and tower spacing, real patrol routes, and barracks
at expansions.
8f23e74155b9:
The top difficulties add a fourth squad for harassment, and wire the handicap
multipliers into the existing axes rather than inventing new ones.
4981e326c177:
Per-squad composition table. Left empty, each squad simply uses the global
composition; filling it in changes behaviour without touching code.
4399105f9c2d:
Machine-learned strategy brain: a single linear policy, used only at the top
difficulties, argmax so it adds no randomness, and able to fall back to the
rule-based brain.
It outputs one of six strategic modes plus a three-way investment split, mapped
onto the existing actuators. The weights are data -- trained offline, loaded as a
file -- so the game itself contains no training code.
dffd2c4dd727:
Intelligence layer: how urgently something needs a look, as a score competing in
the same demand pool as everything else.
Base intelligence changes slowly and stays fresh for a long time (it decides
whether to bring siege); army intelligence changes fast and goes stale quickly
(it decides composition). "Take one more look before attacking" falls out of that
on its own: the base picture is still good, the army picture has expired.
77cea3f53339:
Sustainment cap: army size is bounded by what the food economy can feed --
income from food gatherers and fishing boats, plus stock amortised over time. So
cutting an AI's food supply has a direct causal effect: income falls, the stock
runs down, the cap slides, reinforcement stops, and the army shrinks through
attrition. It adapts to map size automatically.
1e533e836ac3:
Siege engines: below the top difficulties they follow a standard curve that
climbs with game progress and ignores the enemy; at the top they are driven by
actual intelligence about the enemy's base, within bounds.
8b34a10d4039:
Effective army-size multiplier = fair target size x handicap. The two axes meet
here and only here -- ask this function "how big can this tier's army be" rather
than multiplying by the raw knob anywhere else.
20b1d6bafdc2:
During the development phase the cap never scales with enemy strength. Letting it
do so was measurably worse: the army formed earlier, attacked earlier and traded
itself away, and the extra units ate food and crowded out villagers and age
advancement -- turning scouting into an economic tax.
The answer to the development phase is development. If something really does come
to the door, the state machine switches to defending, and that is when matching
the enemy's size matters.
035178521524:
The AI's intelligence API is a thin wrapper over the single vision gate. It used
to be a second implementation of the same idea, and the two drifted apart -- which
is exactly how the side-bias investigation was misled for weeks.
62d158aec035:
Chokepoint scanning skips anything within six tiles of home: the gaps between
your own buildings are not tactical chokepoints. The staging point is also pushed
off building footprints.
70ec35d9b7a5:
The staging point is the nearest open ground before the first chokepoint; with no
chokepoint it is 60% of the way along the route, and it is always at least twelve
tiles from the enemy centre so it never ends up inside their base.
1ad270fe7967:
Repeat sightings merge naturally: remembered strength is the max of what is
visible now and a decaying memory, not a running sum, so seeing the same army
twice does not count it twice.
dfe7e1b58e2b:
Counter-pivot: once the opponent's civilisation is identified, composition leans
towards what counters their main unit.
59a79db3ca40:
Splitting the army is gated by difficulty and by two safety lines: patrols only
once the home guard is above its line (splitting a guard that is already too thin
makes defence worse, so it does not split), and mission squads only once the main
force is above its own.
93866ff3a56a:
Patrols used to be gated on a fully staffed home guard. The first two guards are
exempt from the economic reservation and arrive at once, but the units that
complete the guard have to pass it -- so during the development phase the guard
sat at two indefinitely, patrols never launched, and losing one guard made any
existing patrol evaporate. The gate is now "the core is in place".
04f8ee7100a7:
Reinforcement group: once the main force has left, new units that would fill its
gaps join a reinforcement group at home instead, and march only once enough of
them have gathered. Without it, each new unit walked to the old rally point alone
and died on the way.
d6967c31b2f5:
Escort valve: siege engines cost three times an infantryman and are fragile, so
more of them than the troops escorting them is just an expensive way to deliver
targets. Production clamps the number wanted to a fraction of the non-siege units
available to escort them -- always leaving room for the first one, so it cannot
deadlock. The easier difficulties skip the valve.
56f40a170fbe:
On the defensive, reinforcements go to the main force first. The guard has only
four slots, and with an enemy army harvesting them at the door, a rigid
guard-then-patrol-then-main order poured every new unit into that leaky bucket:
the main force stayed at zero, no counter-attack was ever possible, and defending
became an absorbing state. The main force is the counter-attack, and the guard
stands at the same rally point anyway.
